<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Komyosan Kofun]][[en:Kōmyōsan Kofun]][[ja:光明山古墳]][[zh:光明山古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55438971]] Wikidata name: Kōmyōsan Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Kōmyōsan Kofun | native_name =光明山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kōmyōsan Kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōmyōsan Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.874414|N|137.819108|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|52|27.89|N|137|49|8.79|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kōmyōsan Kofun'''|光明山古墳||}} is a ''[[Kofun period]]'' [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Yamahigashi neighborhood of [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00004095|title= 光明山古墳|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=March 3, 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōmyōsan Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern [[:en:Tōtōmi Province| Tōtōmi]] with [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano]] and inland reaches of [[:en:Mikawa Province|Mikawa]] runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from [[:en:Hamamatsu Station|Hamamatsu Station]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Anterior rectangular portion to right, posterior circular portion to left File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion :File:光明山古墳出土 円筒埴輪・朝顔形埴輪.JPG|''Haniwa'' from the Kōmyōsan Kofun File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-2.jpg|''Fukiishi'' in situ </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Komyosan Kofun]][[en:Kōmyōsan Kofun]][[ja:光明山古墳]][[zh:光明山古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55438971]] Wikidata name: Kōmyōsan Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Kōmyōsan Kofun | native_name =光明山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kōmyōsan Kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōmyōsan Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.874414|N|137.819108|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|52|27.89|N|137|49|8.79|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kōmyōsan Kofun'''|光明山古墳||}} is a ''[[Kofun period]]'' [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Yamahigashi neighborhood of [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00004095|title= 光明山古墳|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=March 3, 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōmyōsan Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern [[:en:Tōtōmi Province| Tōtōmi]] with [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano]] and inland reaches of [[:en:Mikawa Province|Mikawa]] runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from [[:en:Hamamatsu Station|Hamamatsu Station]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Anterior rectangular portion to right, posterior circular portion to left File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion :File:光明山古墳出土 円筒埴輪・朝顔形埴輪.JPG|''Haniwa'' from the Kōmyōsan Kofun File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-2.jpg|''Fukiishi'' in situ </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

= 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|52|27.89|N|137|49|8.79|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kōmyōsan Kofun'''|光明山古墳||}} is a ''[[Kofun period]]'' [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Yamahigashi neighborhood of [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00004095|title= 光明山古墳|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=March 3, 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōmyōsan Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern [[:en:Tōtōmi Province| Tōtōmi]] with [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano]] and inland reaches of [[:en:Mikawa Province|Mikawa]] runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from [[:en:Hamamatsu Station|Hamamatsu Station]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Anterior rectangular portion to right, posterior circular portion to left File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion :File:光明山古墳出土 円筒埴輪・朝顔形埴輪.JPG|''Haniwa'' from the Kōmyōsan Kofun File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-2.jpg|''Fukiishi'' in situ </gallery>

The {{nihongo|'''Kōmyōsan Kofun'''|光明山古墳||}} is a ''[[Kofun period]]'' [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Yamahigashi neighborhood of [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00004095|title= 光明山古墳|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=March 3, 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōmyōsan Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern [[:en:Tōtōmi Province| Tōtōmi]] with [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano]] and inland reaches of [[:en:Mikawa Province|Mikawa]] runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from [[:en:Hamamatsu Station|Hamamatsu Station]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.

It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern [[:en:Tōtōmi Province| Tōtōmi]] with [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano]] and inland reaches of [[:en:Mikawa Province|Mikawa]] runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from [[:en:Hamamatsu Station|Hamamatsu Station]].<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.

==Overview== The Kōmyōsan Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt= Tōtōmi}} with {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}} and inland reaches of {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|WD=Q768158|lt=Mikawa}} runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato Kingdom}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from {{ill|Hamamatsu Station|en|Hamamatsu Station|WD=Q603640|lt=Hamamatsu Station}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Anterior rectangular portion to right, posterior circular portion to left File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion :File:光明山古墳出土 円筒埴輪・朝顔形埴輪.JPG|''Haniwa'' from the Kōmyōsan Kofun File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-2.jpg|''Fukiishi'' in situ </gallery>

The {{nihongo|'''Kōmyōsan Kofun'''|光明山古墳||}} is a ''[[Kofun period]]'' [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Yamahigashi neighborhood of [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00004095|title= 光明山古墳|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=March 3, 2021}}</ref>

The {{nihongo|'''Kōmyōsan Kofun'''|光明山古墳||}} is a ''[[Kofun period]]'' [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Yamahigashi neighborhood of [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00004095|title= 光明山古墳|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=March 3, 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōmyōsan Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt= Tōtōmi}} with {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}} and inland reaches of {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|WD=Q768158|lt=Mikawa}} runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato Kingdom}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from {{ill|Hamamatsu Station|en|Hamamatsu Station|WD=Q603640|lt=Hamamatsu Station}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Anterior rectangular portion to right, posterior circular portion to left File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion :File:光明山古墳出土 円筒埴輪・朝顔形埴輪.JPG|''Haniwa'' from the Kōmyōsan Kofun File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-2.jpg|''Fukiishi'' in situ </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|WD=Q6573285|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline|Komyosan Kofun}} *[https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/shitei/tenryu/tenryu/komyosan.html Hamamtsu city official home page] {{in lang|ja}}

The {{nihongo|'''Kōmyōsan Kofun'''|光明山古墳||}} is a ''[[Kofun period]]'' [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Yamahigashi neighborhood of [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00004095|title= 光明山古墳|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=March 3, 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōmyōsan Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt= Tōtōmi|1=Tōtōmi Province}} with {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano|1=Shinano Province}} and inland reaches of {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|WD=Q768158|lt=Mikawa|1=Mikawa Province}} runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato Kingdom|1=Yamato period}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from {{ill|Hamamatsu Station|en|Hamamatsu Station|WD=Q603640|lt=Hamamatsu Station|1=Hamamatsu Station}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Komyosan Kofun]][[en:Kōmyōsan Kofun]][[ja:光明山古墳]][[zh:光明山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Komyosan Kofun]][[en:Kōmyōsan Kofun]][[ja:光明山古墳]][[zh:光明山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Komyosan Kofun]][[en:Kōmyōsan Kofun]][[ja:光明山古墳]][[zh:光明山古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Kōmyōsan Kofun | native_name =光明山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kōmyōsan Kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōmyōsan Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.874414|N|137.819108|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|52|27.89|N|137|49|8.79|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kōmyōsan Kofun'''|光明山古墳||}} is a ''[[Kofun period]]'' [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Yamahigashi neighborhood of [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00004095|title= 光明山古墳|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=March 3, 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōmyōsan Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt= Tōtōmi|1=Tōtōmi Province}} with {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano|1=Shinano Province}} and inland reaches of {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|WD=Q768158|lt=Mikawa|1=Mikawa Province}} runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato Kingdom|1=Yamato period}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from {{ill|Hamamatsu Station|en|Hamamatsu Station|WD=Q603640|lt=Hamamatsu Station|1=Hamamatsu Station}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Anterior rectangular portion to right, posterior circular portion to left File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion :File:光明山古墳出土 円筒埴輪・朝顔形埴輪.JPG|''Haniwa'' from the Kōmyōsan Kofun File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-2.jpg|''Fukiishi'' in situ </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|WD=Q6573285|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)}}

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Kōmyōsan Kofun | native_name =光明山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kōmyōsan Kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōmyōsan Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.874414|N|137.819108|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|qid=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|52|27.89|N|137|49|8.79|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Kōmyōsan Kofun'''|光明山古墳||}} is a ''[[Kofun period]]'' [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Yamahigashi neighborhood of [[:en:Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu|Tenryū-ku, Hamamatsu]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|qid=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref name= "Bunka1">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00004095|title= 光明山古墳|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=March 3, 2021}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōmyōsan Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It is located at the tip of a tongue-shaped hill of the [[Tenryū River]] plain in western Shizuoka prefecture, and is one a group of several tumuli. The cluster is named for Kōmyō-ji, a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in the vicinity.<ref name= "Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://www.city.hamamatsu.shizuoka.jp/bunkazai/koumyousannkohun.html|title= 光明山古墳が新たに国史跡に指定されます|trans-title= Kōmyōsan Kofun is newly designated as a National Historic Site|language=Japanese |publisher=Hamamatsu City|accessdate=March 3, 2022}}</ref>
The tumulus was discovered in 1939, and was made a Shizuoka Prefectural Historic Site in 1955. An [[archaeological excavation]] was conducted from 2017 to 2018. The tumulus is orientated to the south, and has an overall length of 83 meters, making it the largest in Hamamatsu City. The anterior circular portion has a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.5 meters and is constructed in two tiers. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' have been found on the surface of the mound, and the tumulus's unusual in that ''the fukiishi'' on the north side of the posterior circular portion remained in almost perfect condition with a height of about 6.8 meters from the base to the top of the mound. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] remains undiscovered, as the main body of the mound was not excavated. The construction period is estimated to be around the middleof the 5th century, or the middleof the Kofun period based on the shape of the mound and the characteristics of the ''haniwa''.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
This is the only known ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in the Tenryū region. The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|qid=Q1048864|lt= Tōtōmi|1=Tōtōmi Province}} with {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano|1=Shinano Province}} and inland reaches of {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|qid=Q768158|lt=Mikawa|1=Mikawa Province}} runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato Kingdom|1=Yamato period}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from {{ill|Hamamatsu Station|en|Hamamatsu Station|qid=Q603640|lt=Hamamatsu Station|1=Hamamatsu Station}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Komyosan Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Anterior rectangular portion to right, posterior circular portion to left File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|Posterior circular portion :File:光明山古墳出土 円筒埴輪・朝顔形埴輪.JPG|''Haniwa'' from the Kōmyōsan Kofun File:Komyosan Kofun, kouenbu-2.jpg|''Fukiishi'' in situ </gallery>

The Akiba Kaidō, an ancient highway connecting southern {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|qid=Q1048864|lt= Tōtōmi|1=Tōtōmi Province}} with {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano|1=Shinano Province}} and inland reaches of {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|qid=Q768158|lt=Mikawa|1=Mikawa Province}} runs immediately adjacent to the tumulus. The design of the mound is common to that of the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]] of Japan, an dit is assumed to be the tomb of a local king who controlled this transportation route, and who had some connection with the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato Kingdom|1=Yamato period}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The tumulus is located a ten-minute walk from the "Kōmyōguchi" bus stop on the Entetsu Bus from {{ill|Hamamatsu Station|en|Hamamatsu Station|qid=Q603640|lt=Hamamatsu Station|1=Hamamatsu Station}}.<ref name= "Bunka2"/>
The Kōmyōsan No.2 Kofun located to the south was am {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of 33 meters, but it has now been destroyed.
